title: The Republic
subtitle: Πολιτεία (Politeia) — On Justice, Dialogues Concerning the Ideal State
author: Plato

recommendedTranslation.title: The Republic
recommendedTranslation.note: Highly readable Penguin Classics translation with an excellent introduction, notes, and a thorough glossary. The most accessible modern English edition for first-time readers.

alternativeTranslations.note: Revised edition of the classic Grube translation. Clear, accurate prose used widely in undergraduate philosophy courses; preserves Plato's argumentative structure.
Literary, idiomatic translation that captures Plato's dramatic vigor. Includes a substantial interpretive essay and is favored by political theory and humanities readers.

alternativeTranslations.publisher: Loeb Classical Library / Harvard University Press
alternativeTranslations.note: Definitive two-volume bilingual edition (Greek/English) of the complete Republic. The standard scholarly reference for over a century.
The classic Victorian translation that shaped generations of English-speaking readers. Outdated phrasing but historically indispensable.
Modern Oxford translation with a strong introduction. Strikes a good balance between accuracy and readability for general readers.

themes: The Four Cardinal Virtues (Wisdom, Courage, Temperance, Justice)
Five Forms of Government (Timocracy, Oligarchy, Democracy, Tyranny, Kallipolis)
Justice at the Individual and State Level
The Role of Women and Families in the Ideal State
Poetry, Censorship, and the Just Society
Immortality of the Soul and the Myth of Er
excerpt: Plato's masterwork, written in Athens around 380 BC, is a Socratic dialogue in ten books that asks one of philosophy's most enduring questions: what is justice? Through Socrates' extended conversation with Glaucon, Adeimantus, Polemarchus, and Thrasymachus at the house of Cephalus, Plato builds an ideal city (kallipolis) in speech to discover justice in the human soul. Along the way he introduces the Theory of Forms, the Divided Line, the Allegory of the Cave, the philosopher-kings, the tripartite soul, the noble lie, the four corrupt regimes, the censorship of poets, and the Myth of Er. A foundational text of Western philosophy that has shaped political theory, ethics, metaphysics, and education for two and a half millennia — read, debated, and reinterpreted by everyone from Aristotle and Augustine to Rousseau, Mill, Popper, and Rawls.
